title: LC Reference Samples — Listen to a Conversation

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Did the repair shop call you back about your laptop?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Not yet. They said the replacement keyboard would arrive this afternoon.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: How are you managing without a laptop?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: The shop lent me one of theirs, so I can keep working. They'll text me when mine's ready.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: That sounds really convenient.

questions.stem: What will the woman use while waiting?

options.A: A laptop from the shop
options.B: Her friend's tablet
options.C: The campus computer lab
options.D: Her phone for basic tasks

questions.explanation: The woman says 'The shop lent me one of theirs' — she is using a loaner laptop from the repair shop.

questions.stem: How will the woman know her laptop is ready?

options.A: She will call the shop
options.B: She'll receive a text message
options.C: The shop will send an email
options.D: She needs to check in person

questions.explanation: The woman says 'They'll text me when mine's ready' — she will be notified by text message.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Need anything from the supermarket?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Huh? Aren't we getting ready to go see that play in a few minutes?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That's tomorrow.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Oh. Wow, I'd forget my head if it wasn't screwed on... Guess I don't need to change my clothes after all.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: So, you weren't planning to prepare dinner?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: No, but I can. What do you want?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Just something light and healthy. So, can you go shopping instead?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Yeah, sure. How about salmon and salad? Want anything else?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: No, that's good. Thanks!

questions.stem: What does the man now need to do?

options.A: Get ready for the play
options.B: Change his clothes
options.C: Go shopping
options.D: Call the restaurant

questions.explanation: After realizing the play is tomorrow, the man agrees to go shopping at the supermarket to get ingredients for dinner.

questions.stem: What did the man initially forget?

options.A: He forgot to buy groceries
options.B: He forgot about the timing of their plans
options.C: He forgot to make dinner reservations
options.D: He forgot where the supermarket is

questions.explanation: The man thought the play was tonight, but it's actually tomorrow. He confused the timing of their plans.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Hi, I was hoping to reserve one of the small study rooms on the second floor for tomorrow afternoon. I have a virtual interview for my linguistics thesis, and I need a quiet place.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Well, the standard study rooms on the second floor aren't actually soundproof. If you're doing an interview, you'll probably want to use one of the new acoustic pods on the ground floor instead.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Acoustic pods? I didn't even know we had those.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: We just installed them over the weekend. They're entirely enclosed and specifically designed to block out background noise for video calls and language testing.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That sounds perfect! How do I book one? Do I just write my name on the clipboard outside the door like with the regular rooms?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Not for the pods. They're managed through the university's main facility app. You just log in with your student credentials, select 'Ground Floor Pods', and pick an available time slot.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Okay, I'll download the app right now. Is there a time limit? My interview will probably run for about an hour and a half.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: The maximum booking is two hours per day, so you'll be completely fine. Just be sure to bring your phone with you; the app generates a digital key to unlock the pod door.

questions.stem: Why does the woman approach the man?

options.A: To complain about noise in the library
options.B: To ask for help finding a quiet space
options.C: To report a broken study room door
options.D: To return an overdue library book

questions.explanation: The woman needs a quiet place for a virtual interview and approaches the staff member for help booking a study room.

questions.stem: What will the woman need to enter the pod?

options.A: A physical key from the front desk
options.B: Her student ID card
options.C: A digital key from a mobile application
options.D: A reservation confirmation email

questions.explanation: The man says 'the app generates a digital key to unlock the pod door' — she needs her phone with the app.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Excuse me, do you know if this printer is working? I hit print on my sociology paper a few minutes ago, but nothing has happened yet.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh, that one is really temperamental. Is there an error message on the small screen?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: It says "Tray 2 Empty," but I opened the drawer and there is plenty of paper inside. I tried closing it again, but the light is still flashing red.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That usually means the sensor is jammed or broken. I wouldn't try to fix it yourself. Last semester, I tried to pull a jammed page out of there and ended up getting toner all over my sweater.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Yikes, I definitely don't want that. I have class in twenty minutes.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: You could try the printer in the student union building next door, or just ask the lab assistant at the front desk to take a look.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: I think I'll just ask the assistant. I don't want to run all the way to the student union and find out that printer is busy too. Thanks for the warning!

questions.stem: What is the man's problem?

options.A: He cannot find the computer lab
options.B: He is unable to print his paper
options.C: He lost his sociology assignment
options.D: He does not have enough paper

questions.explanation: The man sent a print job but the printer is malfunctioning — it shows 'Tray 2 Empty' despite having paper, and the light flashes red.

questions.stem: What will the man most likely do next?

options.A: Try to fix the printer himself
options.B: Go to the student union building
options.C: Ask the lab assistant for help
options.D: Print the paper at home instead

questions.explanation: The man says 'I think I'll just ask the assistant' — he decides to ask the lab assistant rather than going to the student union.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Not again!

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Did the lights shut off on you in the study carrel again?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Yeah, it's the third time in an hour. I'm just trying to read for my history seminar, but every twenty minutes it goes pitch black. I have to stand up and wave my arms around like I'm directing traffic just to get them back on.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I know, it's frustrating. The university installed those new motion-sensor lights in the library stacks over the weekend. It's part of the new campus energy conservation plan.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Look, I'm all for saving electricity, but the sensors in this section must be set to a really short timer. If you're sitting quietly reading, it just assumes the aisle is empty.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Actually, the facility manager mentioned they can adjust the timer on the sensors. Since it's a new system, they're still working out the kinks. If you fill out a quick feedback card at the circulation desk, they'll increase the delay before the lights shut off.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Oh, that's great to know. I'll definitely do that on my way out. I can't keep doing jumping jacks just to finish my reading.

questions.stem: What is the conversation mainly about?

options.A: A complaint about noisy students in the library
options.B: An issue with the new energy-saving lighting system
options.C: A request to extend library operating hours
options.D: A discussion about an upcoming history exam

questions.explanation: The conversation centers on the newly installed motion-sensor lights that shut off too quickly, which is part of the campus energy conservation plan.

questions.stem: What will the man most likely do?

options.A: Move to a different section of the library
options.B: Complain to the university president
options.C: Submit a feedback card at the circulation desk
options.D: Install a desk lamp in his study carrel

questions.explanation: The man says 'I'll definitely do that on my way out' referring to filling out a feedback card at the circulation desk.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Hi, I'm hoping you can help me. I found this warning sticker on my bicycle this morning. It says my bike is going to be impounded by the end of the week.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh, I see. Yes, campus security has been tagging unregistered bikes this week. Did you register your bike with the transportation office when you moved into the dorms?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: No, I didn't know I had to. I just brought it from home a few days ago because walking to the engineering building was taking too long.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That explains it. The university implemented a mandatory bike registration policy at the start of the semester to help prevent theft and manage rack space. It's actually free to register.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Oh, well that's a relief. How do I go about doing that?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: You can fill out this form right here. I'll need the make, model, and serial number of your bike. And since you're in the engineering program, you might be interested in renting a spot in the new secure bike lockers near that building.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Secure lockers? That sounds great. My roommate had his bike stolen last year, so I've been a little worried about leaving it outside all day.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Exactly. The lockers are twenty dollars a semester, and they protect your bike from the weather, too.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: I'll definitely do that. Let me just run outside and check my serial number so we can get this sorted out.

questions.stem: Why does the man visit the office?

options.A: To report a stolen bicycle
options.B: To address a warning sticker on his bicycle
options.C: To ask about campus shuttle routes
options.D: To complain about parking fees

questions.explanation: The man found a warning sticker saying his bike would be impounded and came to the transportation office to resolve it.

questions.stem: Why is the man interested in the bike lockers?

options.A: To protect his bicycle from theft
options.B: To store his helmet and gear
options.C: To avoid the registration requirement
options.D: To save money on bus fare

questions.explanation: The man mentions his roommate's bike was stolen last year and he's worried about leaving his bike outside, implying theft prevention is his main motivation.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Hi, excuse me. I reserved a group study room for three o'clock, but there are people in there right now.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Let me look into that for you. Which room did you reserve?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Room 412. I have my confirmation email right here on my phone.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Okay, let's see. You're Sarah, right? Hmm, I don't see your name on the schedule for room 412 today.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Really? But the email definitely says three to five o'clock.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Ah, I see the issue. Looking at your screen, you booked room 412, but it's for the Engineering Building, not the Central Library. They use the exact same booking software, so it's a really common mistake.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh no! My project partners are already on their way here. Is there any space available in this building?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: We are completely booked up for the afternoon. But the Engineering Building is just a five-minute walk across the quad.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I guess I'd better text them to change course. Thanks for catching that!

questions.stem: Why does the woman approach the staff member?

options.A: To extend her study room reservation
options.B: To resolve an issue with an occupied study room
options.C: To report a problem with the booking software
options.D: To ask for directions to the Engineering Building

questions.explanation: The woman reserved a room but found people already using it, so she approaches the staff to resolve the conflict.

questions.stem: Why does the man mention the booking software?

options.A: To explain why the woman booked the wrong building
options.B: To suggest the software needs to be replaced
options.C: To help her cancel the existing reservation
options.D: To warn her about a system outage

questions.explanation: The man mentions the shared booking software to explain how the woman accidentally booked a room in the Engineering Building instead of the Library.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Hey Sarah, did you manage to reserve a room in the library for our study group tonight?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I did, but we might want to rethink our plan to spread out all our notes and textbooks.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Why? Did they put us in one of those tiny interview booths again?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: No, the opposite. The reservation system assigned us the new interactive media lounge on the fourth floor.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: The media lounge? I thought that was just for screening films or practicing presentations.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Apparently, if the regular rooms are fully booked, the system rolls you over to whatever is available. The thing is, there aren't any actual desks in there. It's just a giant projector screen and a dozen bean bag chairs.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Wow. Trying to solve calculus equations while sinking into a bean bag sounds... challenging.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Exactly. I mean, we could project our graphing software onto the big screen to look at the functions together. But writing out the problem sets is going to be tough.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Let's just meet at the student union cafe instead. I'd rather deal with the noise than try to balance a notebook on my knees for two hours.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Good call. I'll cancel the room so someone who actually wants to watch a movie can use it.

questions.stem: Why is the assigned room problematic for the study group?

options.A: It is too small for the group
options.B: It lacks suitable furniture for written work
options.C: The projector is broken
options.D: It is located in a noisy area

questions.explanation: The room has only bean bag chairs and no desks, making it unsuitable for writing out calculus problem sets.

questions.stem: Why does the woman mention the graphing software?

options.A: To suggest a potential advantage of the room
options.B: To convince the man to keep the reservation
options.C: To recommend a new study technique
options.D: To explain why they need a computer lab instead

questions.explanation: The woman acknowledges one positive aspect — they could use the projector for graphing software — but concludes the room is still impractical overall.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Hey Mark, you look out of breath. Did you run all the way to the science building?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Almost! I actually took one of those new green e-bikes from the dorms. The ride itself was fantastic, really fast. But returning it was a nightmare.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Really? I thought the whole point of the bike-share program was convenience.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: It is, until you get to your destination and the docking station is completely full. I couldn't lock the bike anywhere near the science building.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh, right. Everyone heads to the science quad at this time of day. What did you do?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: I had to pedal all the way to the library to find an empty dock, and then sprint back here. I barely made it to my lab on time.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: You know the campus mobility app has a live map, right? It shows exactly how many bikes and empty docks are at each station in real time.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Wait, seriously? I only used the app to unlock the bike. I didn't even look at the other features.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Yeah! Next time, check the map before you leave. It'll save you a lot of running around.

questions.stem: What was the man's problem with the bike-share program?

options.A: The e-bike ran out of battery
options.B: He could not unlock the bike
options.C: There were no available spots to return the bike
options.D: The docking station was too far from the dorms

questions.explanation: The man's problem was that the docking station near the science building was completely full, so he couldn't return the bike there.

questions.stem: What will the man likely do differently next time?

options.A: Walk to the science building instead
options.B: Check the app for dock availability before departing
options.C: Ask a friend for a ride
options.D: Leave earlier to find parking

questions.explanation: The woman suggests using the app's live map feature, and the man seems interested — he will likely check dock availability in advance next time.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Have you noticed those new green racks outside the student union?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Yeah, the umbrella share program. It's a brilliant idea, especially since the rainy season is starting.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I thought so too! Have you actually used one yet?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: I tried to yesterday when it was pouring, but the rack was completely empty.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Really? I read in the campus paper that they stocked them with over two hundred umbrellas. People are supposed to return them to any rack within twenty-four hours.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Well, I think the honor system is the issue. There's no penalty if you just forget to bring it back.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh, I see. Since there's no ID swipe required, people might just be treating them as freebies.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Exactly. They really need to integrate the racks with our student cards. Otherwise, the whole program is basically just a one-time giveaway.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That's a good point. The student council sponsored the initiative, so maybe I'll suggest that at their next open meeting.

questions.stem: What is the conversation mainly about?

options.A: A flaw in a new campus initiative
options.B: Plans to start a recycling program
options.C: A complaint about campus facilities
options.D: A disagreement about student fees

questions.explanation: The conversation revolves around the umbrella share program and its fundamental flaw — the honor system allows people to take umbrellas without returning them.

questions.stem: What does the man think should change about the program?

options.A: Require student ID card scanning
options.B: Charge a rental fee for each umbrella
options.C: Reduce the number of umbrella racks
options.D: Limit usage to rainy days only

questions.explanation: The man says 'They really need to integrate the racks with our student cards' — he wants an ID-based tracking system.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Hi, excuse me. I'm trying to return this reusable takeout container, but the return station by the door doesn't seem to be working. It won't open.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Oh, right. We actually disabled the automated return stations this morning. We're upgrading to a completely app-based system next week.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Oh, I didn't realize. I was just trying to get my deposit token back so I can grab lunch.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: We're skipping the tokens during the transition. If you just hand the container to me, I can manually credit your account. You won't need a physical token anymore.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: That's actually a relief. I lost my token twice last semester and had to pay a fee to replace it.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: You aren't the only one. That's the main reason we're switching. Once the app launches on Monday, you'll just scan a code on the box with your phone.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Sounds much simpler. I'll keep an eye out for that.

questions.stem: Why does the man approach the staff member?

options.A: To purchase a new reusable container
options.B: To get assistance returning a container
options.C: To complain about the food quality
options.D: To ask about the lunch menu

questions.explanation: The man is trying to return his reusable container but the return station is not working, so he approaches the staff member for help.

questions.stem: What is an advantage of the new app-based system?

options.A: It offers a larger food selection
options.B: It prevents the problem of losing physical tokens
options.C: It allows students to order food online
options.D: It reduces the cost of containers

questions.explanation: The man mentions losing tokens twice and paying fees, and the staff says that's 'the main reason we're switching' — the app eliminates the physical token problem.
